blocks.text: If $z$ is a function of $x$ and $y$, i.e., $z = f(x, y)$, the total differential $dz$ is obtained from the partial differentials $dx$ and $dy$ by the use of the following relation:

blocks.latex: dz = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} dx + \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} dy
blocks.description: Total differential of a function of two variables.

blocks.text: The reason for this is more clearly seen if we work from the fundamental idea of change, and introduce the actually measurable quantities like $x$ and $y$.

blocks.caption: Figure 21
blocks.description: This figure illustrates a point P on the surface described by the function z, with coordinates (x, y) and changes in position represented. It shows how small changes in x and y affect the value of z.
This equation represents the chain rule for functions of two variables.

blocks.text: The change in $z$ due to a change in $x$ can be measured by the product of the change in $x$ multiplied by the rate at which $z$ changes with respect to $x$; and this fact can be further illustrated by referring to a diagram (Fig. 21).

Let P be a point $(x, y)$ on a surface, and let P move to a new position Q near to P on the surface. The change of position is most simply expressed as the change from P to Q on the surface (y being kept constant).
